attributes.stem:
An urgent review is requested for a 45-year-old man on the surgical ward due to new fever and agitation. He was admitted three days with a fractured ankle requiring operative fixation. He has a history of heavy alcohol use, depression, and anxiety. His regular medications include sertraline 100mg daily. In addition, he has been receiving regular analgesia on the ward and was also given olanzapine 5 mg orally two hours ago for worsening agitation.

On examination, he appears distressed and confused. His pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 170/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. He is diaphoretic and tremulous. He reports seeing insects crawling on the walls.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features classic for delirium tremens, occurring typically 48-72 hours after cessation of alcohol intake in a patient with alcohol dependence. The presentation includes confusion, visual hallucinations, autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, fever), and agitation.

Delirium tremens is a medical emergency with a mortality rate of up to 5% if untreated. Risk factors include a history of heavy alcohol use, previous episodes of withdrawal, and concurrent illness or injury (in this case, surgery). The onset timing of 3 days post-admission is typical.

Sepsis could present with fever and tachycardia, but would not typically cause visual hallucinations or hypertension. Whilst it would be important to rule out an infective cause of the fever, this is less likely than alcohol withdrawal given the constellation of symptoms present.

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome can occur after administration of antipsychotics like olanzapine. However, the single low dose given recently would be unlikely to cause this condition, and the symptoms began before olanzapine administration. Additionally, NMS typically presents with lead-pipe rigidity and altered consciousness rather than visual hallucinations.

Thyroid storm typically presents with hyperthermia, tachycardia, and agitation, but would usually have additional features such as diarrhoea, vomiting, and a history of thyroid disease. Visual hallucinations would be unusual.

Serotonin syndrome could be considered given the patient is taking sertraline. However, while it can cause autonomic instability and agitation, it typically presents with additional features such as clonus, hyperreflexia, and myoclonus, which are not present here. Additionally, the timing and progression of symptoms is more consistent with alcohol withdrawal.
learningPoints: Delirium tremens typically occurs 48-72 hours after last alcohol consumption and is characterised by confusion, hallucinations, and autonomic instability.
Risk factors for delirium tremens include heavy alcohol use, previous withdrawal episodes, and concurrent illness or injury.
Early recognition and treatment of alcohol withdrawal is essential to prevent progression to delirium tremens.
Multiple medical conditions can present with similar features of autonomic instability and altered mental status, requiring careful medication history.
references: Rahman A, Paul M. Delirium Tremens. [Updated 2023 Aug 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482134/
Grover S, Ghosh A. Delirium Tremens: Assessment and Management. J Clin Exp Hepatol. 2018 Dec;8(4):460-470. doi: 10.1016/j.jceh.2018.04.012. Epub 2018 May 5. PMID: 30564004; PMCID: PMC6286444.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
